A typo'd `champion-3q-line-v1` passes every check — it is not caught by looksSensitive() (regex requires email/phone/`line[_\s-]?user[_\s-]?id`; "champion-3q-line-v1" matches none) and all apply-mode guards (--apply/--append/--confirm-real-data, example-file detection) are satisfied by `npm run import:manual:apply` = `node scripts/import-manual-conversions.mjs --input=data/manual_conversions.csv --output=data/lp_events.jsonl --append --apply --confirm-real-data` (package.json line 95), so the script appendFile()s to data/lp_events.jsonl and prints ok:true. (2) The funnel twin proves intent and asymmetry: import-funnel-aggregates.mjs line 90 builds `knownAssetIds` from config and lines 283-285 `if (!knownAssetIds.has(assetId)) throw new Error(...unknown asset_id...)` inside normalizeAggregateRow, which runs BEFORE its appendFile — same input is blocked pre-write there. (3) Downstream consequence is exactly as claimed: event-input-quality-gate.mjs lines 133-135 emit `unknown_asset_id` for any asset not in config (config/growth-loop.config.json only defines champion-3q-line-v0 and challenger-week0-cta-text-v1, so a v0/v1 mix-up is highly plausible), and line 183 sets `scoring_allowed: issues.length === 0` — one bad line flips it false for the whole store; growth-loop.mjs gates on `eventInputQualityStatus.scoring_allowed === true` (lines 3438, 4346), blocking weekly scoring. No cleanup/rollback tool exists for the append-only jsonl, so recovery is manual line deletion. The typo'd events keep occurred_at/counts, so silent data corruption is also possible if the id happens to collide with a future config asset. Not one of the accepted risks. Severity medium is fair: no security/PII impact, requires an owner typo in a hand-edited CSV (a realistic input for this exact workflow), but it poisons the real event store after reporting success and stalls the scoring pipeline until manual surgery.

confirmed.summary: looksSensitive() omits the 13-19-digit card-number pattern that the funnel twin includes (import-funnel-aggregates.mjs line 406), so payment-card-like values pass the pre-write PII scan and are persisted to repo artifacts.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Owner pastes a wrong spreadsheet column so a card number like `4111 1111 1111 1111` lands in the campaign or content_id cell of data/manual_conversions.csv. Even in default preview mode (no --apply needed) the importer writes it into data/manual_conversions.preview.jsonl, and with apply into data/lp_events.jsonl — violating the hard invariant that no payment PII is ever written to artifacts. The same input is blocked before any write by the funnel importer, and the downstream quality gate only detects it after the PII is already on disk.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED by code comparison and a live reproduction. In /Users/mac/Documents/Codex/control-center/3q-growth-loop/scripts/import-manual-conversions.mjs, looksSensitive() (lines 380-390) contains only three patterns — email, TW phone (`/(?:\+?886[-\s]?)?0?9\d{2}[-\s]?\d{3}[-\s]?\d{3}/`), and `line_user_id` — while the funnel twin scripts/import-funnel-aggregates.mjs line 406 additionally has `if (/\b(?:\d[ -]*?){13,19}\b/.test(normalized)) return true;` (card-number pattern). The pre-write gate at manual importer lines 113-123 blocks only when looksSensitive fires, and `campaign`/`content_id` are allowed columns not exempted, so a card number passes. I reproduced the exact failure: a CSV row with campaign=`4111 1111 1111 1111` run through the manual importer in default preview mode (no --apply) exited 0 with status `ok: true, contains_sensitive_values: false` and wrote `"campaign":"4111 1111 1111 1111"` into the output JSONL — ironically stamped `"no_pii": true` in metadata_json. The same value tested against the funnel twin's card regex returns true (blocked before write). With --apply --append --confirm-real-data the same value would be appended to data/lp_events.jsonl (write at lines 128-132, after the only sensitive-value check). This is not among the accepted risks (collector no-auth, fixtures, deploy-3q-site.yml). Severity medium is apt: requires operator paste error, but defeating the pre-write PII scan is exactly the guard's purpose and the sibling importer proves the intended behavior.

confirmed.summary: Fill templates prefill a single date (current Taipei Monday, week.start) on every row while instructing the owner to 'Fill only aggregate counts', collapsing a whole week of data onto one noon timestamp so the min_test_days=3 sample gate reports unmet even when data genuinely spanned 3+ days.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Owner collects Mon-Sun real data, fills the count column of funnel_aggregates.fill-template.csv exactly as the report's rules instruct (line 272: 'Fill only aggregate counts'), copies and applies it. Every expanded event lands at 2026-XX-XXT12:00:00+08:00 (+1s increments) on the same Monday, so growth-loop's calculateTestDays(first,last) returns 1; sample_threshold_met stays false on test_days despite 7 days of real data meeting every other threshold, blocking the champion/challenger decision for at least one extra weekly cycle ('gate blocks when the underlying condition IS met'). The template offers no per-day rows, so multi-day attribution is impossible without violating the written instructions, and daily attribution in all downstream artifacts is permanently wrong (everything stamped Monday).
verify_notes: Confirmed end-to-end by tracing the actual code. (1) Template generation: scripts/real-data-input-pack.mjs lines 38-39 call `buildRows(..., week.start)` and line 210 stamps `date` (the current Taipei Monday from `currentTaipeiWeek`) on EVERY row of funnel_aggregates.fill-template.csv — verified in the generated file: all rows read `2026-07-06,...`. There is no per-day row structure, and the written owner instructions are count-only: status line 82 "Fill the count column with reviewed aggregate numbers, then copy to data/funnel_aggregates.csv" and report line 272 "Fill only aggregate counts." — the report contains zero guidance about editing dates. (2) Import expansion: scripts/import-funnel-aggregates.mjs `normalizeDate` (line 350) converts `YYYY-MM-DD` to `${raw}T12:00:00+08:00` and `expandAggregateRow` (line 328) emits `occurred_at: addSeconds(row.occurred_at, index)` — so every expanded event lands on that same Monday at 12:00:00+08:00 plus at most count-1 seconds (count is capped at 10000 ≈ 2.8h, cannot cross a day). (3) Gate: scripts/growth-loop.mjs `calculateTestDays` (line 5993) is span-based: `Math.floor((end - start) / 86400000) + 1`, so all-same-day events yield 1; line 1857 requires `testDays >= thresholds.min_test_days` (config min_test_days=3) for `sample_threshold_met`, and line 1898 sets challenger decision to "keep_testing_sample_insufficient" when it is false — blocking the champion/challenger decision even when visits/cta/line_add thresholds are met from 7 days of genuinely collected data. The flow is realistic and first-class: the pack's own report BLUF calls it "the Week 0 real-data handoff" and lists the exact copy + `npm run import:funnel:apply`-family + `npm run week0` follow-ups. Decisive corroboration that this is a defect rather than intended semantics: the project's own decision-replay fixtures (scripts/real-data-decision-replay.mjs lines 447, 561-565) deliberately `splitCount(count, counts.test_days)` across `addDays(startDate, dayIndex)` — i.e., the authors know per-day dates are required to pass the gate, yet the owner-facing template provides only one date and the instructions forbid touching anything but counts. Caveat that keeps severity at medium (not high): the gate fails closed (keep testing, no wrong promotion), a workaround exists (owner edits dates or uses the sample_gate_ledger path whose per-row `capture_date` compiles into per-day dates via source-capture-compile.mjs line 274), and if live D1 telemetry events with real timestamps also flow into lp_events.jsonl for the same asset the multi-day span could mask the gate impact — but daily attribution of the template-derived events would still be permanently wrong (all stamped Monday noon). This is not one of the accepted risks.

confirmed.summary: The win-rule fixture suite validates a scoreAssets re-implementation whose line_add_rate denominator (`link_clicks || visits`) contradicts the deployed worker (`visits || link_clicks`, src/index.ts:406) and the visits-denominator invariant, and every scenario sets link_click == page_view so the suite can never detect the divergence.
confirmed.failure_scenario: win_rule_fixture_status.json reports ok=true and the report says the win rule is validated, yet on any asset pair where link_clicks != visits (the normal production shape, since only routed traffic emits link_click) this rule implementation produces a different line_add_rate and can flip the promotion decision relative to the worker. The gate certifies 'win rule pass' while the underlying rule it certifies mis-scores real data — a consumer trusting this status as verification of the >1.15 rule is misled.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED. scripts/win-rule-fixtures.mjs:324-325 computes `const denominator = row.link_clicks || row.visits || 0; const lineAddRate = safeDivide(row.line_adds, denominator)` (link_clicks-first), while the deployed worker at src/index.ts:460 computes `row.line_add_rate = roundNumber(safeRatio(row.line_adds, row.visits))` (strictly visits-only), guarded by an explicit comment (lines 453-459) that the rate "must stay visits-only" because "only routed traffic emits link_click" — i.e. the fixture contradicts the worker's stated invariant even more strongly than claimed (worker doesn't even fall back to link_clicks for line_add_rate; the claim's line-406 citation is off, actual is 455/460, but the substance holds). All 6 fixture scenarios set link_click == page_view (120/120, 99/99, ...), so the two denominators always coincide in the suite and the divergence is structurally undetectable — the suite always reports ok=true. On production-shaped data (link_clicks < visits) the fixture's rule yields different line_add_rates and can flip the 1.15 promotion decision (e.g. champion 20 adds/100 clicks/200 visits vs challenger 13/100/100: worker lift 1.30 = eligible, fixture rule lift 0.65 = not eligible). The status IS consumed as verification: goal_completion_audit.md:94 marks it local_verified, launch_readiness.json check `win_rule_fixtures` / `win_rule_fixture_ok: true`, growth-loop.mjs:5044 `present_win_rule_fixture_passed`. This is not the accepted 'fixtures fabricate local data' risk — it's a stale oracle: the worker's denominator was fixed (the audit's 分母 hole) but the fixture kept the pre-fix logic, so the 'win rule pass' certifies a rule that differs from the deployed one. Medium severity is apt: the deployed worker itself scores correctly; the defect is a misleading verification gate.

confirmed.summary: The contract audit hardcodes ok:true, so partial live-observation failures still produce a passing status whose report and downstream gates claim the champion URL and LINE destination were 'verified read-only'.
confirmed.failure_scenario: During a weekly run the https://lin.ee/... redirect fetch times out (or /health fails) while /contact still loads: fetchObservation returns ok:false for that probe, liveReadOk becomes false and status.status becomes 'live_observation_incomplete', but status.ok stays true and the process exits 0. renderReport's BLUF still prints 'The live champion URL and LINE destination are verified read-only', and downstream consumers gate only on ok===true plus observation flags (growth-loop.mjs buildApprovalQueue championLineOnlyObserved at ~line 2799, objective-sequence-audit.mjs championContractAuditCheck at ~line 2332, verify-artifacts.mjs line 838) and never read live_read_ok — so the owner approval queue and objective audit report a verified LINE-only contract even though the LINE destination observation actually failed.
verify_notes: Confirmed by tracing the code. Line 39 of scripts/champion-contract-audit.mjs hardcodes `ok: true` while line 36 computes `liveReadOk = [health, landing, contact, line].every((item) => item.ok)` and only the string field degrades (`status: !liveReadOk ? "live_observation_incomplete" : ...`). fetchObservation catches timeouts and returns `{ ok:false, ... }` instead of throwing (lines 120-121), so main() resolves and the process exits 0. In the claimed scenario (LINE redirect probe fails, /contact loads), lineOnlyContactDetected is computed solely from contactHtml, so renderReport's BLUF (lines 151-155, which never consults live_read_ok) prints "The live champion URL and LINE destination are verified read-only. The contact page is LINE-only...". Repo-wide grep confirms no code outside the producer reads live_read_ok or the status string: growth-loop.mjs lines 2805-2807/3155-3157 gate championLineOnlyObserved on `ok === true && line_only_contact_detected === true && misleading_success_state_detected === false` (all true in the scenario), objective-sequence-audit.mjs championContractAuditCheck (line 2466) gates on ok/mode/url/flags and passes, and verify-artifacts.mjs line 883 asserts only `ok === true`. So the approval queue and objective audit report a verified LINE-only contract despite the failed LINE observation. Minor line-number drift in the claim (verify-artifacts 883 vs 838; audit check 2466 vs ~2332) does not affect substance. Not among the accepted risks; medium severity fits an audit-integrity gap with no direct external effect.

confirmed.summary: The release preflight accepts the integration-smoke evidence on smokeStatus.ok===true alone with no binding to the current candidate (the smoke status records no candidate hash and preflight checks no timestamp ordering), so a stale smoke run false-passes the gate after the candidate is rebuilt.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Candidate is regenerated with different bytes (e.g., champion-integration.config.json line_url or telemetry change followed by npm run champion:integration:build), then 'npm run champion:release:preflight' is run standalone without rerunning champion:integration:smoke: data/champion_integration_smoke_status.json still holds ok:true from the previous candidate (buildStatus in champion-integration-smoke.mjs stores no candidate sha256 that preflight or verify-artifacts could cross-check, unlike candidate_source_lock_matches and the local-branch sha bindings), so isolated_two_worker_smoke_ok passes, preflight reports ok:true, and champion_release_owner_packet.md plus champion_github_pr_body.md assert 'isolated champion plus collector smoke passed' for candidate bytes that were never smoke-tested — evidence the owner uses to approve the production deploy.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED (check is at line 158, not 165 — same checks block). scripts/champion-release-preflight.mjs line 158 reads `isolated_two_worker_smoke_ok: smokeStatus.ok === true,` and that is the ONLY use of smokeStatus besides the read at line 29 — no candidate sha256 cross-check, no generated_at ordering vs candidateStatus. The smoke's buildStatus() (champion-integration-smoke.mjs lines 515-575) records collector_url/champion_url/contracts but no hash of the candidate bytes (verified in the live data/champion_integration_smoke_status.json), even though the smoke tests exactly those bytes: integrations/3q-site/wrangler.jsonc has "main": "./generated/worker.candidate.js". build-champion-integration-candidate.mjs writes only the candidate status/report/generated files and never invalidates SMOKE_STATUS_PATH, so after a rebuild (e.g. config line_url change) the stale ok:true survives. package.json exposes "champion:release:preflight": "node scripts/champion-release-preflight.mjs" as a standalone entrypoint, so the scenario needs no unusual state. verify-artifacts.mjs (lines 933-951) asserts smoke content but never binds it to the current candidate sha256 or timestamps, so nothing closes the gap. Result: preflight reports ok:true and the owner packet plus champion_github_pr_body.md (line 28: "isolated champion plus collector smoke passed") assert smoke coverage for never-tested bytes — false owner-approval evidence. Deploy remains owner-gated and the full `npm run verify` chain happens to run smoke right before preflight, which caps this at the claimed medium severity rather than high. The contrast the reviewer draws is real: candidate evidence is hash-bound via candidate_source_lock_matches (lines 152-157) and local-branch candidate_sha256, while the smoke evidence has no binding at all.

confirmed.summary: scanObjectForSensitiveData never inspects array contents (arrays fail both the object-recursion branch due to !Array.isArray and the typeof === 'string' value check), so sensitive values or objects nested inside arrays in metadata_json pass the PII gate undetected.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Empirically verified: a line in data/lp_events.jsonl with metadata_json {"contacts":["0912-345-678","someone@example.com"]} produces zero issues -> status.ok=true, scoring_allowed=true, pii_or_sensitive_data_detected=false, and scoring proceeds over a file containing a Taiwan phone number and an email address; {"list":[{"email":"a@b.c"}]} likewise escapes because the recursion never enters the array.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED by code trace and by executing the actual script in an isolated copy. The recursion branch at line 280 is `if (value && typeof value === "object" && !Array.isArray(value)) { scanObjectForSensitiveData(...) }` — an array fails `!Array.isArray(value)`, and the fallback check at line 284 is `typeof value === "string"`, which an array also fails, so array elements (strings or nested objects) are never visited. No other path covers it: `scanObjectForSensitiveValues` (line 290-298) explicitly skips the field with `if (key === "metadata_json") continue;` and only checks top-level string values anyway, and `normalizeMetadata` returns the object without scanning. Empirical run of scripts/event-input-quality-gate.mjs (copied with its config into scratchpad so the real data/event_input_quality_status.json was untouched) against a fixture containing exactly the claimed lines — metadata_json {"contacts":["0912-345-678","someone@example.com"]} and {"list":[{"email":"a@b.c"}]} — produced ok:true, issues:[], scoring_allowed:true, pii_or_sensitive_data_detected:false, exit code 0, even though looksSensitiveValue() would flag both "0912-345-678" (Taiwan mobile regex, line 309) and the emails (line 308) if they were reached, and isSensitiveKey("email") would flag the nested key. The key "contacts" matches no SENSITIVE_KEY_PATTERNS entry, so nothing else trips. metadata_json is free-form JSON from the telemetry pipeline, so arrays are realistic input, and the gate's stated purpose ("Blocks scoring on ... sensitive customer data") is silently defeated — consistent with medium severity. This is not one of the accepted-risk items (collector no-auth, fixtures, deploy-3q-site.yml).

confirmed.summary: renderCommand emits an unguarded `open "$ROOT/<path>"` line for every target (including ones the launcher itself just detected as missing) into a `set -eu` zsh script, so the first missing file aborts the owner launcher partway and silently skips all remaining review artifacts.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Any one of the 50 open-targets is absent when OPEN-3Q-GROWTH-LOOP.command is generated or later double-clicked (e.g. a partially failed weekly run that never regenerated north_star_outcome_form_fixture_report.md, or an artifact archived/deleted after generation). The launcher still writes the .command unconditionally (line 537) even when it sets ok:false/missing_targets. When the owner double-clicks it, `open` on the missing file exits 1 (verified on macOS) and `set -eu` (line 573) aborts the script, so every target listed after the missing one — prepared_but_blocked.md, approval_queue.json, worker_dry_run.md, owner_quality_review_form.html, etc. — never opens and the trailing 'Opened local files:' confirmation never prints; the owner reviews only a truncated subset of the decision surface believing they launched the full set. Fix is to filter on target.exists or guard each line with `[ -f ... ] &&`.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED by code trace and live execution test. renderCommand (scripts/owner-action-launcher.mjs:549-551) emits `open "$ROOT/${target.path}"` for every target with no existence guard — `targets.map((target) => `open "$ROOT/${target.path}"`)` — even though each target carries a computed `exists` flag (line 328) that renderCommand ignores (the report renderer at line 615 does use it). The generated script starts with `set -eu` (line 573) and the open block (line 602) precedes the "Opened local files:" confirmation (lines 605-606). The .command is written unconditionally at line 537 before the `if (!status.ok)` check at 543, which only sets exitCode and never suppresses the write, so a run that itself records missing_targets still ships unguarded open lines for those files. Empirically verified on this macOS machine: a zsh `set -eu` script running `open` on a nonexistent file prints "The file ... does not exist.", `open` exits 1, and the script aborts immediately — subsequent opens and the trailing confirmation never execute (observed exit code 1, later lines never printed). All 48 targets exist right now (claim said 50; actual OPEN_TARGETS count is 48 — immaterial), so the bug is latent, but the scenario is realistic: the code explicitly models missing targets, and the persistent double-clickable .command outlives generation, so any later-archived/never-regenerated artifact truncates the owner's review set with only a single easily-missed error line. Medium severity matches: silent truncation of the owner decision surface, no data loss, trivial fix (filter on target.exists or `[ -f ... ] &&` guard).

confirmed.summary: buildAutoPasteTemplate/buildPartialAutoPasteTemplate hand-roll a synthetic template containing a single comma-free comment line instead of exercising the template renderPasteTemplate actually generates, so scenarios 'auto_paste_template_preview_ready', 'partial_auto_paste_template_waiting', and 'labelled_counts_file_preview_ready' all pass while the real generated-template flow is completely broken (see the parseCountsText comment-splitting bug).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified by execution: node scripts/next-p0-quick-capture-fixtures.mjs reports ok:true with all 9 scenarios green, yet running quick-capture against its own generated template — fully and correctly filled — always yields blocked_invalid_quick_counts; the fixture reports the auto-paste-template gate as working when the underlying condition is not met, so the weekly guard never surfaces the broken owner workflow.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED by execution. (1) Root cause: parseCountsText in scripts/next-p0-quick-capture.mjs splits BEFORE comment-checking — line 318 `for (const item of raw.split(/[,\n;]+/))` then line 320 `if (!text || text.startsWith("#")) continue;` — so only the first fragment of a comment line keeps its "#". The real renderPasteTemplate (lines 479-499) emits comment lines containing both delimiters: line 483 "...all counts are complete; otherwise run manually:" (semicolon) and line 485 "# Keep this aggregate-only: no phone, email, LINE user ID, customer name, chat text, order ID, payment ID, or private note." (six commas). (2) Reproduced end-to-end in a temp dir: generated the real template via the script itself, filled every <count> and all metadata placeholders correctly, re-ran → status "blocked_invalid_quick_counts", filled_preview_created:false, counts_source "auto_paste_template", with unknown_ranks exactly the comment fragments: ['invalid:otherwise run manually:', 'invalid:email', 'invalid:LINE user ID', 'invalid:customer name', 'invalid:chat text', 'invalid:order ID', 'invalid:payment ID', 'invalid:or private note.']. So the documented owner workflow (auto-read weekly path and the accepted --counts-file=<paste template> format, report lines 565-566) can never succeed. (3) Fixture gap as claimed: buildAutoPasteTemplate/buildPartialAutoPasteTemplate (fixtures lines 239-261) hand-roll a template whose only comment is the delimiter-free "# 3Q Growth Loop Next P0 Paste Counts Template", and labelled_counts_file_preview_ready writes bare labelled pairs with no comments — none exercise renderPasteTemplate's actual output. Existing data/next_p0_quick_capture_fixture_status.json shows ok:true with all 9 scenarios green, so the weekly guard reports the auto-paste-template gate healthy while the real flow is 100% broken. This is not the accepted "fixtures fabricate local test data" risk — the defect is that the fixture's synthetic input structurally diverges from production output and masks a real parser bug. Severity medium is fair: it silently blocks the owner quick-capture path but stays local (soft-block, exit 0, no data corruption or external effect).

confirmed.summary: The paste template is preserved only when readAutoCountsFile finds at least one strictly-valid integer count (hasFilledCount); if the owner has filled only the metadata lines (evidence_ref/reviewer/pii_checked) or wrote counts in an invalid format, the next run regenerates the template from placeholders and silently destroys the owner's edits, contradicting the documented 'Weekly runs preserve partial edits'.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Verified by execution: generate template, replace evidence_ref/reviewer/pii_checked with real values but leave counts as <count>, rerun the script (as the weekly runner does) -> hasFilledCount returns false, readAutoCountsFile returns null, preservePasteTemplate stays false, and writeFile overwrites the template — evidence_ref/reviewer/pii_checked revert to <aggregate_ref>/<alias>/<yes_after_aggregate_only_review>, wiping the owner's work with no warning; same wipe occurs if every count was typed in a non-integer format.
verify_notes: Confirmed by code trace and live execution. readAutoCountsFile (scripts/next-p0-quick-capture.mjs:284) returns null unless hasFilledCount finds at least one strictly-integer count value — hasFilledCount (lines 302-313) skips metadata lines ('if (metadataKey(match[1])) return false;') and requires /^(0|[1-9]\d*)$/ on the value, so a template with only evidence_ref/reviewer/pii_checked filled (counts still <count>) or with all counts in non-integer format yields null. readCounts line 253 then sets preservePasteTemplate=false, and main line 99 ('if (!countsResult.preservePasteTemplate) await writeFile(PASTE_TEMPLATE_PATH, renderPasteTemplate(...))') regenerates the template from placeholders with no warning. Reproduced in a sandboxed run: filled evidence_ref/reviewer/pii_checked=yes, left counts as <count>, reran with no args — all three reverted to <aggregate_ref>/<alias>/<yes_after_aggregate_only_review> and status reported paste_template_preserved:false. The trigger is routine: scripts/weekly-runner.mjs:278 runs 'npm run next-p0:quick' with no counts flags (and the package.json verify chain does too), hitting exactly this path. It directly contradicts the template's own header (line 483: 'Weekly runs preserve partial edits...') and the report text (line 566: 'partial owner edits are preserved'). Medium severity fits: silent loss of owner-typed local edits, recoverable only by retyping; not one of the accepted-risk exclusions.

confirmed.summary: capture_date is validated only as YYYY-MM-DD format and never checked against the expected capture week (nextP0.week is loaded from the same JSON but unused), while candidatePaths() auto-picks a never-archived, never-freshness-checked ~/Downloads/next_p0_owner_inputs.filled.csv on every run, so wrong-week or stale aggregates validate as 'next_p0_owner_download_preview_ready' and can be staged into data/funnel_aggregates.csv / data/manual_conversions.csv.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Owner downloaded next_p0_owner_inputs.filled.csv two weeks ago and left it in ~/Downloads (the intake never consumes/renames it); with the inbox empty and quick preview header-only, every weekly run re-validates that stale file (ranks/tracking ids/event types still match the round definition, capture_date passes the format regex) and reports preview-ready with next_safe_action suggesting staging; after the human runs --stage --confirm-owner-reviewed, rows dated in an already-scored completed Taipei week (or dated after week_end, e.g. counts captured Monday for the prior week) flow via import-funnel-aggregates into occurred_at, where the weekly window filter silently drops them or attributes them to the wrong week, skewing line_add_rate comparisons — the invariant-4 window is never enforced at the only validation gate that sees the data.
verify_notes: Confirmed by code trace. (1) Line 274 of scripts/next-p0-owner-intake.mjs is the only capture_date check: `!/^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$/.test(row.capture_date.trim())` — format only. (2) next_p0_owner_inputs.json carries `"week": {"start":"2026-07-06","end":"2026-07-12"}` but the script uses nextP0 solely via `nextP0.inputs` (lines 124, 193); all other row checks are week-agnostic equality against the round definition, so a weeks-old file passes. (3) Line 29/431: `~/Downloads/next_p0_owner_inputs.filled.csv` is auto-picked when the inbox is empty and quick preview is header-only; the script has no rename/unlink/archive and no mtime check, so the same stale file re-validates as `next_p0_owner_download_preview_ready` (statusName, line 384) every run, with nextSafeAction (line 399) suggesting `--stage --confirm-owner-reviewed`, which writes data/funnel_aggregates.csv and data/manual_conversions.csv (maybeStage lines 373-374). (4) Downstream, import-funnel-aggregates.mjs maps date→occurred_at (`${raw}T12:00:00+08:00`, lines 298/350) with no week bound, and growth-loop.mjs scoreAssets counts ALL lp_events.jsonl events with no occurred_at window filter — `week_start: week.start` (line 1862) merely stamps the current Taipei week onto rows, so stale rows are attributed to the wrong week and skew line_add_rate (lines 1848/1865) and champion/challenger decisions. Minor imprecision: the claimed "window filter silently drops them" branch doesn't exist (there is no filter at all — only wrong-week attribution occurs), but that is the same defect stated in the claim's core: the expected capture week is never enforced at any gate. No downstream freshness check exists (event-input-quality-gate.mjs, real-data-intake-plan.mjs have none). Medium severity fits: human-confirmed local staging, no external effect, but realistic leftover-Downloads state corrupts weekly A/B scoring.

confirmed.summary: Ledger rows are deduplicated into a Map keyed by tracking_link_id::stage with last-row-wins and no duplicate detection, so a duplicated CSV row silently shadows the owner-filled one (or silently accepts whichever conflicting count comes last).
confirmed.failure_scenario: Owner fills a row (aggregate_count=150, all metadata valid) but the ledger also contains a later stale/empty duplicate of the same tracking_link_id+stage (e.g. Excel edit left the original template line in place) -> verified by execution: ledger_row_count=19 vs expected 18, the filled row is discarded, the key reports state=pending with empty count, and issue_count=0 / warning_count=0 -- the discrepancy is never flagged; in the reverse order two conflicting counts pass with the last silently chosen, while the downstream source:compile reads the raw CSV and may aggregate differently from what the gate validated.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED by code reading and execution. Line 87 of scripts/sample-gate-batch-preflight.mjs builds `const rowsByKey = new Map(parsed.rows.map((row, index) => [rowKey({ tracking_link_id: row.tracking_link_id, event_type: row.stage }), { row, row_number: index + 2 }]))` — the JS Map constructor silently keeps the LAST entry per duplicate key, and nothing anywhere detects duplicates (ledger_row_count is emitted at line 160 but never compared to expected_p0_row_count; the unexpected-row loop at lines 130-139 iterates the already-deduped Map and only warns on keys absent from expectedByKey, so a duplicate of an expected key can never warn). Reproduced with the repo's real sample_gate_batch_handoff.json (18 all_rows) and real fill-template: (A) filled row with aggregate_count=150 + later stale empty duplicate of the same tracking_link_id+stage -> output: ledger_row_count=19 vs expected_p0_row_count=18, target key reports row_number=20 (the empty dup), state=pending, aggregate_count="", issue_count=0, warning_count=0 — the owner's filled row is silently discarded and never flagged, exactly as claimed. (B) two conflicting counts (150 then 200) -> state=ready with count=200, issue_count=0, warning_count=0 — last silently wins. Downstream divergence also confirmed: scripts/source-capture-compile.mjs compileRows (lines 249-293) iterates ALL raw rows via rows.forEach with no key dedup and sums every valid filled row into countsByEventType (line 285: `(result.countsByEventType[outputRow.event_type] ?? 0) + count`), so in scenario B compile would aggregate 150+200=350 while the gate validated only 200. Realistic input (leftover template line after Excel edit), silent wrong gate verdict in a data-quality preflight, consequence proportionate to medium severity; not among the accepted risks.

confirmed.summary: Fixture scenarios hardcode absolute dates for the week of 2026-07-06 but invoke sample-gate-due-status.mjs without --next-p0/--config overrides, so the week under test comes from the live next_p0_owner_inputs.json and the guard falsely fails in every other week.
confirmed.failure_scenario: growth-loop.mjs (line 173) regenerates next_p0_owner_inputs.json with currentTaipeiWeek(now) on each weekly run; from 2026-07-13 the live week becomes 2026-07-13..19, so scenario day3_due (--today=2026-07-08) computes minCheckDate=2026-07-15 and classifies waiting_until_day3 instead of day3_due_waiting_for_owner_counts -- day3_due, day3_overdue_recovery and day7_due checks all fail, status.ok=false and the fixture exits 1, permanently blocking the weekly/CI guard even though the due-status logic is correct (a fixture that should be hermetic is coupled to mutable live state).
verify_notes: CONFIRMED by code trace and empirical reproduction. (1) The fixture (scripts/sample-gate-due-fixtures.mjs lines 129-139) passes --today/--progress/--owner-sample-gate/--capture-calendar/--owner-action but NOT --next-p0 or --config, so the child falls back to the live file: sample-gate-due-status.mjs line 7 `const NEXT_P0_PATH = path.join(ROOT, "next_p0_owner_inputs.json")` and line 43 `const week = nextP0.week ?? currentWeek(generatedAt, timezone);` — the week under test comes from mutable live state (and the ?? fallback is the *current real week*, so it drifts either way). (2) The live week does move: growth-loop.mjs line 173 `const week = currentTaipeiWeek(now);` feeds buildTrackingLinks (line 177) -> tracking_links.json -> data-collection-brief.mjs line 43 `const week = trackingLinks.week ?? ... ?? currentTaipeiWeek(generatedAt)` -> data_collection_queue.json -> data-collection-progress.mjs line 79 `week: dataQueue.week` and line 144 writes next_p0_owner_inputs.json. Live file today holds week 2026-07-06..12, exactly matching the fixture's hardcoded scenario dates (2026-07-07/08/09/12) with config min_test_days=3/preferred_test_days=7. (3) Empirical repro: running sample-gate-due-status.mjs with --today=2026-07-08 against a next-p0 file whose week is 2026-07-13..19 returns status=waiting_until_day3, min_check_date=2026-07-15, due_now=false — the fixture expects day3_due_waiting_for_owner_counts/due_now=true, so status_matches/phase_matches/event_matches/due_now_matches all fail; day3_overdue_recovery and day7_due fail the same way (todays 07-09 and 07-12 are all < 07-15). scenario.ok=false -> status.ok=false -> fixture line 83 `if (!status.ok) process.exitCode = 1;`. (4) Consequence matches severity: package.json `verify` chain includes `npm run sample-gate:due:fixtures`; weekly-runner.mjs step sample_gate_due_status_fixtures (line 355) rejects on nonzero exit (`reject(new Error(\`${command.step} failed with exit code ${code}\`))`, line 1174) setting process.exitCode=1; and verify-artifacts.mjs lines 2030-2031/2130 assert the fixture must stay in both chains, so it cannot be silently dropped. From the week starting 2026-07-13, every weekly/verify run fails until scenario dates or overrides are fixed. This is not the accepted "fixtures fabricating local test data" risk — the defect is the opposite: a fixture meant to be hermetic is coupled to regenerated live state.

confirmed.summary: summarizeRows sums every valid row with no duplicate-row or template-row-count check (and source-capture-compile has none either), so an accidentally duplicated ledger row silently inflates visits/CTA/LINE totals and can report sample_threshold_met when true counts are below the gate.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Owner hand-edits data/source_capture/sample_gate_ledger.filled.csv per the worksheet and accidentally pastes one challenger page_view row (aggregate_count=50) twice, giving 19 rows; validateRow passes each row, rows_read is never compared to the 18-row template, visits become 149 instead of 99, min_visits=100 is reported as met, and status flips to sample_rate_win_needs_quality_review even though the real sample is insufficient — no issue or warning is emitted anywhere in the compile+owner-gate chain.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED by executing the actual script. summarizeRows (owner-sample-gate-status.mjs lines 265-268: `if (row.stage.trim() === "page_view") asset.visits += count;` etc.) sums every validateRow-passing row with no duplicate-row check; validateRow (305-341) is strictly per-row so a byte-identical duplicate passes; rows_read (line 154) is captured but never compared to the 18-row template; only the `dates` Set dedupes (protecting test_days, not counts). Reproduction with the real script and config (min_visits=100, template=18 rows): clean 18-row ledger with challenger visits=99 yields status=sample_insufficient_keep_champion / sample_threshold_met=false; the same ledger with one challenger page_view row (aggregate_count=50) pasted twice (19 rows) yields issue_count=0, warning_count=0, challenger visits=149, sample_threshold_met=true, ok=true, status=sample_rate_win_needs_quality_review — exactly the claimed silent flip, no issue or warning anywhere. Grep confirms source-capture-compile.mjs, owner-sample-gate-intake.mjs, and sample-gate-owner-form.mjs have no dedup or row-count guard either, and the documented workflow is owner hand-editing this CSV then running npm run owner:sample-gate, so the input is realistic. Medium severity fits: no auto-promotion occurs (decision=queue_owner_quality_review_no_auto_promotion), but the sample-sufficiency gate — the script's entire purpose — is silently defeated and inflated totals flow into the owner review report.

confirmed.summary: countsByEventType merges intake.counts_by_event_type with the preflight-selected source's counts precisely when the selected source IS next_p0_owner_intake, and mergeCounts adds them, so every event count is doubled in owner_sample_count_recovery.json/.md once real counts exist.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Owner fills counts; next-p0-owner-intake writes counts_by_event_type {page_view:220, cta_click:48, line_add:12} (countBy sums the count column), owner-data-preflight selects source next_p0_owner_intake whose counts_by_event_type is computed from the same preview rows with the same sums; mergeCounts adds both, and the recovery artifact reports 'Counts by event: {page_view:440, cta_click:96, line_add:24}' — exactly double the owner's actual aggregates. The bundled fixtures never set intake counts_by_event_type, so they cannot catch it.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED double-count. Line 219-222: `mergeCounts(intake.counts_by_event_type ?? {}, preflightFull.selected_source_id === "next_p0_owner_intake" ? selectedCounts(preflightFull) : {})` adds two copies of the same aggregates. Evidence: (1) next-p0-owner-intake.mjs:163 writes intake status `counts_by_event_type: countBy(previewRows, "event_type", "count")` (sums count column) AND writes those same previewRows to data/next_p0_owner_intake/*.owner-preview.csv (lines 128-134). (2) owner-data-preflight.mjs SOURCES (lines 20-25) reads exactly those preview CSVs for source id `next_p0_owner_intake` and computes `counts_by_event_type: countBy(rows, "event_type")` where countBy (lines 642-647) does `acc[row[key]] += row.count` — identical sums over identical rows, preserved into owner_data_preflight.json source_statuses via stripSourceRows (line 634). (3) selectSource (lines 211-224) picks next_p0_owner_intake precisely when its previews have rows (live files absent pre-staging, the normal recovery state), which is precisely when intake counts are nonzero — so the ternary guarantees both merge inputs are populated with the same numbers. (4) mergeCounts (lines 432-440) is pure addition, so owner_sample_count_recovery.json line 255 and the MD "Counts by event" line 510 report exactly 2x the owner's actual counts. Fixture gap confirmed: owner-sample-count-recovery-fixtures.mjs intakeStatus() hardcodes counts_by_event_type:{} (line 484) with no override, so the suite never exercises both-nonzero. Severity medium is apt: no gate decision consumed (thresholds come from preflight directly), but the owner-facing recovery artifact fabricates doubled aggregates in a pipeline whose core promise is honest owner-sourced counts.

confirmed.summary: The secret-value detector's regex requires a hyphen after the token prefix (e.g. 'ghp-'), but the real token formats it names use underscores ('ghp_', 'gho_', 'github_pat_', and Stripe 'sk_live_'), so raw secrets pasted into free-text evidence fields pass validation undetected.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Owner pastes a real GitHub PAT into a required free-text field, e.g. owner_gate_evidence.json github gate with verification_ref: "verified via ghp_16AbCdEf0123456789XYZ". isSensitiveField('verification_ref') does not match, and looksSensitiveValue fails because /\b(?:sk|pk|pat|ghp|...)-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{12,}/ demands 'ghp-' with a hyphen. The gate is reported evidence_valid=true, ready_for_post_gate_verification=true, and the status/report assert sensitive_evidence_detected=false, while the live secret sits in the tracked repo artifact owner_gate_evidence.json that the guard claims to have screened. The identical broken pattern exists in scripts/approval-resume-plan.mjs line 186 (looksSecretValue), so the owner-approval-form -> approval-resume-plan round trip misses the same token in e.g. rollback_plan (the browser form's keyword-only check at scripts/owner-approval-form.mjs line 250 also cannot catch a bare token string). Note the fixture owner-approval-form-fixtures.mjs only exercises this path with the field NAME 'api_token' (caught by field-name matching), so the value-level detector's failure is never tested.
verify_notes: Confirmed by executing the actual code. Line 406 of scripts/owner-gate-evidence.mjs reads /\b(?:sk|pk|pat|ghp|gho|github_pat|xoxb|xoxp|cf)-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{12,}/ — the literal hyphen after the alternation means real GitHub tokens (ghp_/gho_/github_pat_, which use underscores) and Stripe sk_live_ all test false (verified with node against the exact regex), while the nonexistent 'ghp-' hyphen form tests true. No other check saves it: isSensitiveField (line 401) does not match 'verification_ref'/'rollback_ref'/'notes_ref'; the Bearer/PEM/email alternatives at 407-409 don't match a bare token; looksPlaceholder (line 570) and validateGateFields (305-379) impose no format constraint on those free-text fields. Result: blockedReasons empty → evidence_valid=true, ready_for_post_gate_verification=true (line 210), and sensitive_evidence_detected=false (line 272) — false assurance from the screening control. Corroborated: identical broken regex in scripts/approval-resume-plan.mjs looksSecretValue (~line 186); owner-approval-form.mjs sensitivePattern (~line 250) is keyword-only and cannot catch a bare token; fixture owner-approval-form-fixtures.mjs line 75 uses field name api_token (caught by name matching) with value ghp_sensitivefixturevalue12345 which the value regex misses — so the value-level path is untested. Two caveats that don't overturn it: owner_gate_evidence.json is gitignored ('tracked repo artifact' is overstated — secret persists on disk but isn't committed), and verification_ref belongs to the d1/deploy/ab gate contracts, not the github gate as in the reviewer's example (github gate would flag it as unknown_fields). Medium severity is apt: a secret detector that misses every token format it names, with false-negative assurance, but mitigated by the gitignore.

confirmed.summary: The quality-review form conflates export validity with quality PASS: values below the retention thresholds or above the spam threshold disable both download buttons, so the form cannot export honest regression evidence that the downstream validator explicitly accepts as valid 'keep champion' input.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Owner measures a true regression (e.g. close_rate_retention_vs_champion = 0.7 < 0.8) and fills the form. validate() pushes 'close_retention_below_threshold' (lines 364-366), sets downloadJson.disabled = true (lines 372-373), and the documented flow ('place the downloaded JSON at data/owner_quality_review.filled.json, then run npm run owner:quality-review') becomes impossible. Yet owner-quality-review.mjs treats exactly this input as VALID (exit 0, status owner_quality_review_failed_keep_champion, quality regression correctly recorded), and the form's own fixture (owner-quality-review-form-fixtures.mjs scenario quality_form_export_regression_keeps_champion, close=0.7) replays a JSON the real form can never produce. Practical effect: the regression outcome can only be recorded by hand-editing JSON outside the form, and the path of least resistance in the UI is to raise the numbers above threshold, which flips the outcome to no_quality_regression=true and promotion_review_queued=true — converting a true quality regression into a queued promotion review.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED by direct code trace. (1) Form: owner-quality-review-form.mjs lines 364-366 push threshold breaches ("close_retention_below_threshold" etc.) into the same `found` array as PII/format errors; lines 372-373 set `downloadJson.disabled = !ok; downloadReview.disabled = !ok;`, and both download click handlers re-validate and `if (!result.ok) return;` (lines 415, 420) — so a measured close_rate_retention_vs_champion = 0.7 (< config threshold 0.8) can never be exported from the form, by any path. (2) Downstream: owner-quality-review.mjs deliberately separates `issues` (invalid input, exit 1) from `qualityFailures` (lines 256-277); sub-threshold values with sample_rate_win_candidate=true return `ok: true` (exit 0), status "owner_quality_review_failed_keep_champion", decision "keep_champion_due_quality_regression" (lines 152-164) — a first-class valid outcome of the exact flow the form's own Hard Rules document ("place the downloaded JSON at data/owner_quality_review.filled.json, then run npm run owner:quality-review"). (3) Contract proof: owner-quality-review-form-fixtures.mjs lines 101-114 define scenario quality_form_export_regression_keeps_champion with input close=0.7 described as "The browser-downloaded JSON is valid but shows quality regression" — a browser download the real form's JS cannot produce. This is not the accepted fixtures-fabricate-data risk; the fixture evidences the intended contract the form violates. Practical effect matches medium severity: honest regression evidence requires hand-editing JSON outside the UI, and the path of least resistance (raising values to >= threshold) flips the downstream outcome to no_quality_regression=true / promotion_review_queued=true (lines 166-176). Not higher than medium because promotion remains a manual red-line gate (no auto-promotion or external effect).

confirmed.summary: rowsByKey is built with new Map(parsed.rows.map(...)), so duplicate (tracking_link_id, stage) rows in the filled ledger are silently collapsed last-wins with no issue or warning, while npm run source:compile sums every duplicate row — the preflight reports ready_for_outcome_source_compile for a batch that will double-count outcomes.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Owner appends a corrected deal row (count=5) below the original mistaken row (count=3) instead of editing it. The preflight validates only the last row, emits zero issues/warnings, and sets ready_for_source_compile=true; source-capture-compile.mjs then compiles both rows into the owner-preview CSV (8 deals instead of 5), inflating deal/lead/link_click outcome aggregates that feed the North Star report and weekly scoring inputs.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED by code trace and live reproduction. Line 85 `new Map(parsed.rows.map((row, index) => [rowKey(row), ...]))` with key `tracking_link_id::stage` collapses duplicates last-wins; only the last duplicate is validated (lines 90-121), and the unexpected-row warning loop (lines 123-127) iterates the already-collapsed map so it can never flag a duplicate of an expected key. Reproduced with the real template: filled all rows count=3, appended a corrected deal row count=5 for the same tracking link. Preflight output: status=ready_for_outcome_source_compile, ready_for_source_compile=true, issue_count=0, warning_count=0 (and its own summary shows deal=20, inconsistent with compile). source-capture-compile.mjs then compiled BOTH rows (rows.forEach at line 249, per-row validation only, sums at line 285): counts_by_event_type.deal=23 and the funnel owner-preview CSV literally contains `...,deal,3,...` and `...,deal,5,...` for the same asset/content/variant — 8 deals where the owner meant 5. No downstream dedup exists: import-funnel-aggregates.mjs line 325 expands every row into count events; grep found no duplicate detection in import-manual-conversions, real-data-intake-plan, or north-star-funnel. Medium severity is fair: an owner-review copy-to-live gate exists, but the preflight is the designated gate for this exact data-entry error and greenlights it, then recommends running source:compile → north-star, inflating outcome aggregates. Not among the accepted known risks.

confirmed.summary: Filled-task matching keys on (tracking_link_id, event_type) only and ignores the ledger row's week_start, so a stale prior-week source_capture_ledger.filled.csv / sample_gate_ledger.filled.csv marks the new week's entire collection queue as filled.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Week N: owner fills all rows in source_capture_ledger.filled.csv. Week N+1: the weekly runner regenerates data_collection_queue.json with new week dates but the same link_ids/event_types, and the old filled.csv is still on disk. Every taskRow gets filled=true from last week's counts, status becomes all_collection_rows_filled_review_next, next_owner_inputs is empty, and the owner is told nothing needs collecting for a week that actually has zero captured counts.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED. Line 268 builds filled_keys as taskKey(row.tracking_link_id, row.stage) where taskKey = `${trackingLinkId}|${eventType}` — no week component — even though both the ledger CSV (header: week_start,week_end,...) and every queue task (data_collection_queue.json has week_start/week_end per task) carry week info that is discarded. Task matching at lines 38-39 uses the same week-less key. The failure scenario is the default week-rollover outcome: (1) the filled ledger lives at a fixed path (data/source_capture/source_capture_ledger.filled.csv) that the progress script itself points owners to; (2) nothing ever rotates/deletes it — archive-weekly-run.mjs only copyFile()s and doesn't even list filled.csv, and no script unlinks these ledgers; (3) data-collection-brief.mjs regenerates the queue weekly with link ids from growth-loop.mjs line 1489 `track-${asset.asset_id}` (e.g. track-champion-3q-line-v0), which are stable across weeks, so week N+1 reuses identical (tracking_link_id, event_type) pairs; (4) the package.json verify chain runs data:brief && ... && data:progress weekly, and the earlier source:compile step has no week-freshness check that would abort first. With every key matched from last week's rows (which legitimately pass the aggregate_count and pii_checked=yes filters at lines 258-259), lines 67-71 set status=all_collection_rows_filled_review_next, line 104 leaves next_owner_inputs empty, and the report BLUF claims all rows are filled — telling the owner nothing needs collecting for a week with zero captured counts. Medium severity is accurate: misleading local progress/orchestration output, no live-data corruption. Not one of the accepted-risk exclusions.

confirmed.summary: The form's downloaded CSV contains only the 24 P1 outcome rows (rows are pre-filtered to OUTCOME_EVENTS before rendering/toCsv), yet the HTML hard-rules instruct the owner to place it at the shared data/source_capture/source_capture_ledger.filled.csv — overwriting and silently discarding any P0 page_view/cta_click/line_add rows previously filled in that same file.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Owner fills P0 sample-gate rows in source_capture_ledger.filled.csv (the owner_fill_path every P0 task in data_collection_queue.json points to), then later uses north_star_outcome_form.html and follows its instruction to save the download to that path. The 42-row file is replaced by a 24-row file with no page_view/cta_click/line_add rows; the next source:compile and data-collection-progress run lose the P0 counts with no error, regressing the sample-gate progress and any recompiled funnel preview.
verify_notes: CONFIRMED by tracing the full path. (1) The form embeds only template rows passing `.filter((row) => OUTCOME_EVENTS.includes(row.stage))` with OUTCOME_EVENTS=["link_click","lead_submit","deal","quality_flag"] (north-star-outcome-form.mjs:11,21), and toCsv() iterates only those embedded rows — the download is exactly 24 data rows; the template has 42 (6 each of 7 stages, verified by counting the CSV). (2) Line 315's hard rule unconditionally says "place the downloaded CSV at data/source_capture/source_capture_ledger.filled.csv, then run npm run north-star:outcome-preflight" — a full replace, no merge. (3) The overwrite target is genuinely shared: every P0_sample_gate task (page_view/cta_click/line_add) in data_collection_queue.json has owner_fill_path "data/source_capture/source_capture_ledger.filled.csv", and data-collection-brief.mjs:309 instructs copying the full 42-row template there to fill — so owner-entered P0 counts realistically live in that file. (4) The loss is silent: the instructed preflight only validates the 24 OUTCOME_EVENTS rows (north-star-outcome-preflight.mjs:83 filters template rows to OUTCOME_EVENTS; its report even states P0 rows "stay governed by the sample-gate preflight"), so a 24-row file passes ok; source-capture-compile.mjs compiles whatever rows exist with no completeness check (funnel preview silently drops P0 stages); data-collection-progress.mjs recomputes filled/pending from keys present in the ledger, so P0 tasks revert to pending with ok:true. (5) No guard: the "owner_filled_source_capture_detected_review_before_overwrite" status is only a JSON string, and even the guarded intake (owner-p1-outcome-intake.mjs:201) stages via raw copyFile overwrite. Only mitigation is the progress script's union with sample_gate_ledger.filled.csv, which helps only if the owner duplicated P0 counts there. Consequence (silent local data loss + sample-gate/funnel progress regression, recoverable by re-entry, no production impact) matches the claimed medium severity.

confirmed.summary: The launch_readiness.local_preflight_ok dependency never checks the actual local preflight results; it returns ok when gate.status === "owner_approval_required", which growth-loop.mjs hardcodes on every owner gate, making this dependency always-true.
confirmed.failure_scenario: launch_readiness.json currently has local_preflight_ok=false (weekly_runner preflight failed), yet data/gate_readiness_status.json shows the remote_d1_create_and_migrate dependency 'launch_readiness.local_preflight_ok' as ok=true. The remote D1 gate's only other dependencies are the hardcoded-true schema check and approval metadata, so the moment the owner fills valid non-secret approval metadata, the matrix reports ready_for_owner_execution=true and tells the owner 'Owner may execute this gate manually' while local preflight is failing — a false-ready that defeats the matrix's stated purpose of validating prerequisites. The needed data (context.localPreflight) is already passed into dependencyStatus but unused.
verify_notes: Confirmed by tracing code and live state. Line 183 of scripts/gate-readiness-matrix.mjs returns `ok: context.gate.status === "owner_approval_required"` for the launch_readiness.local_preflight_ok dependency, and growth-loop.mjs hardcodes status "owner_approval_required" on all four owner gates (lines 3711/3754/3795/3815), making the check always-true. The passed-in localPreflight data (destructured line 177, mapped line 178) is unused for this check. Live state proves it: launch_readiness.json has local_preflight_ok=false (weekly_runner preflight ok=false) while data/gate_readiness_status.json shows the dependency ok=true ("launch readiness remains owner-gated"). The remote_d1 gate's other deps are hardcoded-true schema (line 180) and approval metadata, whose readiness in approval-resume-plan.mjs (line 127) validates only metadata fields, never preflight. So filling the non-secret metadata — the matrix's own current next_owner_action — flips ready_for_owner_execution=true and emits "Owner may execute this gate manually" (line 268) despite failing preflight. Medium severity matches: false-ready signal in a prerequisite-validation tool, but human still executes manually and no autorun occurs.

confirmed.summary: Cleanup candidates are selected as the first (count - keep_latest_recommended) items after a lexicographic path sort, not by modified time, so for the logs section the owner-facing deletion guidance flags the NEWEST files as 'older' cleanup candidates and keeps older ones.
confirmed.failure_scenario: Live state confirms it: logs/ has 1186 files with mixed name prefixes; the 1066 flagged candidates in data/artifact_retention_status.json include logs/champion-integration-smoke-20260710T091839Z.log (modified 2026-07-10 09:18 — the newest log in the directory), logs/approval-plan-current-state.log (2026-07-10), and logs/.gitkeep (a tracked repo placeholder), while genuinely older weekly-runner-20260708T2055*.log files land in the 'kept newest 120'. The report and review pack tell the owner 'Keep the newest 120 files and manually review older local-only artifacts' — an owner who follows this and deletes the listed candidates destroys the current day's run logs and .gitkeep while retaining stale ones. newest_item/oldest_item are mislabeled by the same path-sort assumption (oldest_item is currently .gitkeep).
verify_notes: CONFIRMED by code trace and live-state reproduction. Code: scripts/artifact-retention-monitor.mjs line 149 sorts logs lexicographically by path — `files.sort((a, b) => a.path.localeCompare(b.path));` — then buildSection line 163 takes the FIRST items as cleanup candidates: `const cleanupCandidates = items.slice(0, cleanupCount)`, and lines 196-197 label `newest_item: items.at(-1)` / `oldest_item: items[0]` from that same path order. mtime is collected (line 224) but never used for ordering. For timestamped-name sections (bundles, archive) path order coincides with time, but logs/ has mixed prefixes, so path order != time order. Live state confirms every detail: data/artifact_retention_status.json shows logs oldest_item = `logs/.gitkeep` (a tracked placeholder, mislabeled oldest and flagged as a cleanup candidate), and `logs/approval-plan-current-state.log` (modified 2026-07-10T03:30, current day) is in the candidate preview. Reproducing the exact selection over the live logs/ dir (1224 files): today's newest logs — champion-integration-smoke-20260710T091839Z.log (09:18) and tracking-link-smoke-20260710T110408Z.log (11:04, the true newest) — fall in the 'delete-candidate' set, while ALL 120 'kept' files (weekly-runner-/worker-dry-run- from 2026-07-09) are older by mtime than the newest candidate. Owner-facing guidance makes it actionable: artifact_retention_review_pack.json line 210 literally says 'Keep the newest 120 file and manually review older local-only artifacts.', and the status next_safe_action says to 'manually archive or delete old local-only artifacts'. An owner following this deletes the current day's run logs and .gitkeep while retaining stale ones. Severity medium fits: the monitor never deletes anything itself (owner_only_manual_cleanup_after_review), so the harm is misdirected manual cleanup destroying local-only logs — real, currently manifest, but not a production/data-integrity impact. Not one of the known accepted risks.

low_unverified.summary: Malformed or non-object JSON bodies on POST /e are returned as 500 internal_error instead of a 400 client error.
low_unverified.failure_scenario: POST /e with body "{bad json" makes JSON.parse throw SyntaxError, or body "null" (valid JSON) makes sanitizeIncomingEvent throw TypeError reading payload.asset_id; neither message matches the 400 allowlist, so the outer catch logs it as an error and returns 500 internal_error with CORS headers — a pure client-input problem is misclassified as a server failure and pollutes the error log on every malformed beacon.

low_unverified.summary: decodeURIComponent on the raw pathname in /r/:asset (line 162) and /ab/:id (line 489) throws URIError on invalid percent-encoding, turning the request into a 500 instead of 400/404.
low_unverified.failure_scenario: GET /r/%e0 or GET /ab/%zz — the pathname survives URL parsing with the invalid escape intact, decodeURIComponent throws URIError('URI malformed'), the outer catch does not recognize the message, logs it as an internal error, and responds 500 internal_error; the visitor gets no redirect and routine bot scans with mangled escapes generate 500s/error-log noise.

low_unverified.summary: In /r/:asset the link_click insert runs inside ctx.waitUntil, so a FOREIGN KEY failure for an unknown asset_id is swallowed after the redirect and the event is silently lost.
low_unverified.failure_scenario: A marketer publishes a short link with a typo'd asset id, e.g. /r/challanger-week0-cta-text-v1?to=line — every visitor is redirected to LINE normally, but insertEvent rejects on the lp_events asset_id foreign key inside waitUntil (no caller awaits or catches it), so zero link_click rows are recorded for the whole campaign and nothing anywhere surfaces the failure; weekly scoring just sees no clicks.

low_unverified.summary: A malformed POST /e body causes JSON.parse's SyntaxError message — which echoes a verbatim snippet of the request body — to be written to logs, violating the no-PII-in-logs red line.
low_unverified.failure_scenario: A client POSTs a non-JSON body containing PII, e.g. the raw string "email=victim@example.com&phone=0912345678", to the public /e endpoint. bodyText is <8192 chars so it passes the size gate, then JSON.parse(bodyText) at line 190 throws a SyntaxError whose message in workerd/V8 embeds the body verbatim (e.g. `Unexpected token 'e', "email=victim@examp"... is not valid JSON`). That message is not in the ['asset_id_required','invalid_event_type','blocked_metadata_key'] allowlist and does not match the FOREIGN KEY regex, so execution reaches console.log(JSON.stringify({ level: 'error', message })) at line 209, persisting the email/phone snippet into Cloudflare observability logs (the response itself stays generic 'internal_error').

low_unverified.summary: The HTML-breakout guard in injectGrowthLoopTelemetry is a runtime no-op: .replaceAll('<', '<') uses the JS string literal '<', which the engine parses as the '<' character itself, so it replaces '<' with '<' and performs no escaping (the intended six-character escape requires '\\u003c'); the same dead sanitizer is in worker.candidate.patch line 80.
low_unverified.failure_scenario: If env.GROWTH_LOOP_COLLECTOR_URL is ever set (via a bad deploy config, copy-paste error, or compromised CI variable) to a value containing '</script><script>...</script>', JSON.stringify keeps the '<' characters, the no-op guard leaves them unescaped, and the collector URL literal terminates the inline telemetry <script> early — injecting attacker/operator-supplied HTML and script into every HTML page served by the production site, while also breaking the telemetry script; the code that was written specifically to prevent this provides zero protection.

low_unverified.failure_scenario: Owner fills the P0 sample counts and double-clicks RUN-P0-POST-FILL-CHECK.command (its intended use); during the weekly:local step the pipeline regenerates the file with a new stage string, making it longer than the 1170-byte running copy; after the final echo lines, zsh does another read() on the still-open fd, picks up the appended bytes of the new file, and executes the fragment — typically an unmatched-quote parse error with exit code 1 (Terminal shows the post-fill check as FAILED right after it actually succeeded), and if the fragment happens to align on a line boundary it can re-execute a `run_step npm run ...` line, duplicating pipeline steps.

low_unverified.summary: Same self-rewrite-while-running defect as the P0 launcher: `npm run weekly:local` runs owner:p1-outcome-postfill-check (weekly-runner.mjs:243), whose generator (scripts/owner-p1-outcome-postfill-check.mjs:120-121) truncate-writes this running .command file with variable-length regenerated content, so zsh can execute a misaligned tail fragment of the new file after the original script body finishes.

low_unverified.summary: On a failed step, runCommand rejects and discards the already-built result object (status:'failed', exit_code), so the 'failed' status file's commands array omits the failed step and all pending placeholders, and downstream monitors report zero failed commands for a failed run.

low_unverified.summary: After a hard crash leaves the lock behind, isProcessActive treats any reused live PID — including EPERM from an unsignalable other-user process — as the still-running owner, so the scheduled weekly invocation exits 0 with ok:true 'already_running' and the week's run is silently skipped.
low_unverified.failure_scenario: A weekly run is SIGKILLed or the machine loses power (the finally block never runs), leaving the lock with the old PID. Within the next 4 hours (MAX_RUN_LOCK_AGE_MS), macOS reuses that PID for an unrelated long-lived process — or the PID lands on a root-owned daemon, where process.kill(pid,0) throws EPERM and line 1233 returns true. When launchd fires the Sunday 00:10 run, acquireRunLock sees ownerActive=true, main() prints ok:true status:'already_running' and exits 0, performing no commands. launchd sees a clean exit and will not retry until next week, so the entire weekly window is skipped while every immediate signal (exit code, JSON ok flag) reports success; the miss only surfaces later when schedule-catchup-status runs elsewhere and flags missed_weekly_run_owner_review.

low_unverified.failure_scenario: artifact-retention-monitor.mjs hits an unhandled error mid-walk (e.g., EACCES from lstat, which is not caught — only ENOENT in readdir is), so its catch handler writes data/artifact_retention_status.json with status 'failed', ok:false, and no sections array. A subsequent npm run artifacts:retention-review reads that file, gets sections=[], computes reviewRequired=false, and publishes artifact_retention_review_pack.md/.json with ok:true and BLUF 'within_review_budget' — a green health report generated directly from a failed monitor run.

low_unverified.summary: When owner_approval_input.json contains multiple approval entries with the same gate_id, only the first is validated and used (approvals.find), and later conflicting or corrected entries are silently ignored with no duplicate detection or blocked reason.
low_unverified.failure_scenario: Owner hand-edits owner_approval_input.json and appends a corrected entry for public_ab_small_traffic_link below an earlier draft entry whose rollback_url points to the wrong (but syntactically valid) URL. The plan validates only the first draft entry, marks the gate ready_for_owner_execution=true, and gate-readiness-matrix's approved_rollback_url dependency likewise passes based on the stale entry — the resume plan endorses the wrong pending approval item and the owner executing the public-link gate consults the wrong rollback metadata. The mirror case (invalid first entry, valid correction second) silently blocks a gate the owner believes is approved. Only sensitive-value scanning covers all duplicates; field presence, placeholder, and validation checks run solely on the first match.

low_unverified.summary: one_variable_consistent (line 396) and content_variant_tracking_unique (line 398 via contentVariantTrackingCheck at line 3417) pass vacuously when content_variants.json contains zero drafts, because [].every() is true and contentLinks.length === drafts.length holds at 0 === 0, with no check anywhere requiring at least one live draft.
low_unverified.failure_scenario: The variant generator produces content_variants.json with one_variable_rule_ok: true and drafts: [] (e.g. an emptied template list), tracking_links.json has no role === "content_variant" links, and funnel_breakdown.json has no content_variant rows. Both checks pass vacuously (the draft_count >= 3 requirement at line 1933 applies only to the rotation fixture status, not live variants), so the weekly audit reports the one-variable and per-draft-tracking contracts as verified for a round that generated no content drafts at all.

low_unverified.summary: "All checks must pass" assertions of the form Object.values(x.checks ?? {}).every(v => v === true) are vacuously true when the generator omits or empties the checks object, so a generator regression that drops the whole check block passes verification.
low_unverified.failure_scenario: If scripts/build-champion-integration-candidate.mjs regresses and writes data/champion_integration_candidate_status.json without a `checks` object (or with checks: {}), Object.values({}).every(...) returns true and line 914 ("all champion integration candidate checks must pass") passes with zero checks actually verified; no assertion elsewhere requires any specific key inside championIntegrationCandidate.checks or a minimum key count. The same vacuous pattern applies at line 931 (championIntegrationSmoke.page_contract?.checks ?? {}) and line 1052 (championLocalBranch.checks ?? {}), where no follow-up assert pins individual check keys either — unlike lines 1024-1026 (D1 schema) and 1091 (release preflight) which would still catch a dropped object. Result: verify prints ok:true while an entire class of champion-candidate safety checks silently stopped being evaluated.
